evidence_quotes.quote: Video games sadly have been in a legal lacuna for a while, and the EU has been on the forefront legislating in previous years.

Rights covering loot boxes (lottery like reward system) and refund requirements (14 days refund required also for digital items) have been important for customers all over the world and especially EU citizens.

paragraphs.text: Video games sadly have been in a legal lacuna for a while, and the EU has been on the forefront legislating in previous years. Rights covering loot boxes (lottery like reward system) and refund requirements (14 days refund required also for digital items) have been important for customers all over the world and especially EU citizens. There is an issue regarding lifecycle of games though, more specifically the end of life part, that has slipped through the cracks in terms of legislation. Developers of games are not required in any way to keep games alive or allow customers, who bought and paid for access to the game, to keep the game alive after the developer ends support. No one, neither customers nor developers/publishers, expect games(or any service) to be supported indefinitely, but most would agree that a product that has been bought should not be able to remotely be made unusable. If a product requires a service from a company to function, there should be a way for customers, on their own dime, to continue using the product should the company be unable or unwilling to continue it's support. Right now, a game can be sold for 100 euros and developer/publisher can shut down it's support within a year should it not be profitable for them. One can understand that developer/publisher can't continue support if not profitable, but there should be guidelines as to how one can stop support and a framework for how customers can continue using the product without support from the developers/publishers. This legislation should be for future projects, not currently/already existing games, allowing developers/publishers to plan and allocate time/money to such a plan. Outline requirements in legislation, but allow specifics to be up to developers/publishers as the way a game functions will vary greatly between projects. I hope this can be added in addition to the digital rights act, which have been great for citizens and customers both in EU/EU Economic Area and all over the world. Thank you for reading
